video_title: US Crypto Perps Arrive, Solana Rethinks Fees
gist: Blockworks researchers (Danny, Carlos, Kunal) parse three developments: the CFTC's approval of Kalshi's onshore BTC perpetual contract (and Coinbase/Deribit no-action) as the start of US-regulated crypto perps; Solana's proposed SIMD-547 resource-based base fee to restore SOL burn and net-emission reduction tied to network usage; and the Pokemon/TCG mania driving Collector Crypt's on-chain gacha to record ~$9M monthly net revenue, which they frame as a reflexive casino-beta trade rather than a fundamentally cheap token.

insights.claim: The CFTC approved a perpetual BTC futures contract submitted by Kalshi on a roughly one-day turnaround, and Coinbase received a no-action letter letting it offer perps/derivatives via its Deribit acquisition, signaling US incumbents can now list onshore perps.
insights.speaker: Danny
insights.so_what: Onshoring of perps opens a high-margin, high-revenue product line for US-listed venues (Coinbase, Robinhood) and is a near-term revenue catalyst worth tracking.
insights.quote: the CFTC approved a BTC perp contract submitted by Kalshi

insights.claim: The immediate beneficiaries of US perp approval are existing incumbents like Coinbase and Robinhood that already run these products internationally and can simply turn them on for US users, not Kalshi, which lacks an edge in capturing perp flow.
insights.speaker: Kunal
insights.so_what: Trade the incumbents with existing international perp infrastructure for the revenue bump rather than the prediction-market names whose perp ambitions are margin-driven extensions.
insights.quote: the platforms where people go to already trade perps and spot are likely to be the the key benefactors here

insights.claim: Decentralized exchanges like Hyperliquid and Lighter will most likely achieve US compliance via KYC at the front-end/builder-code layer while keeping the base liquidity layer permissionless, avoiding a fragmented low-liquidity 'Binance US' style split.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: Suggests DEX perp liquidity stays unified and competitive in the US, a bullish structural point for Hyperliquid/Lighter versus a feared liquidity-fragmentation outcome.

insights.claim: Roughly 80% of perp volume comes from the top three or four assets, so initial BTC/ETH/SOL listings capture most of the product's value immediately, while exotic 24/7 stock perps and pre-IPO perps face case-by-case CFTC review and may not arrive soon.
insights.speaker: Danny
insights.so_what: Volume/revenue from onshore perps front-loads to majors; pre-IPO and equity perps are a separate, slower regulatory track and a later optionality bet.
insights.quote: 80% of the volume comes from those like top three, four assets anyway

insights.claim: Regulation lags real-world demand: institutions are already watching products like a SpaceX IPO perp trade on Hyperliquid, and retail uses banned venues via VPN, so onshore approval mainly captures untapped institutional flow rather than changing existing retail behavior.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: The incremental TAM from regulation is institutional capital that could not compliantly trade onshore, not the retail base already using offshore/DEX venues.

insights.claim: Solana's SIMD-547 proposal (by Caveman Loverboy/KB of Temporal) adds a resource-based base fee charged on requested compute units, designed to restore SOL burn and reduce net emissions by tying fees to network usage without cutting validator issuance income.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: If passed, it creates a usage-linked SOL burn that can offset inflation and potentially make SOL deflationary at high activity, a structural value-accrual change for the token.
insights.quote: it is attacking the emissions problem for Solana from the the mind side from the demand side

insights.claim: The proposal is engineered to barely affect optimized actors (market-maker Oracle updates rise ~2%) and validators, while charging retail-style transactions more (e.g. a pump.fun swap up ~60%), but since Solana fees are under a cent the increase is negligible to users.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: Politically cleverer than prior failed proposals (SIMD-228) because it spares validator revenue, raising its odds of passing a vote.
insights.quote: market makers have optimized their Oracle updates to have to consume as little CUs or compute units as possible

insights.claim: SIMD-96 (activated Feb 2025) removed the 50% priority-fee burn, collapsing daily SOL burn from ~10,000/day in Q4 2024 to near zero; SIMD-547 could restore a meaningful ~2,000-5,000 SOL/day burn.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: Quantifies the swing in SOL net supply: a restored burn at these levels materially offsets the ~3.8% annualized inflation now diminishing 15%/year.
insights.quote: after February 2025 when SIMD 96 was activated, that essentially went to zero

insights.claim: Because the fee scales with both network usage and the block compute-unit limit (proposed to rise from 60M to 100M and beyond), the burn mechanism would grow with future network capacity, an under-appreciated long-term tailwind.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: Gives SOL a value-accrual story that compounds as the chain scales throughput, distinct from one-off inflation cuts.
insights.quote: this also like allows uh the burn to potentially scale with not just network usage, but also network capacity

insights.claim: The tokenomics debate only reignited because Solana activity and price declined from Q1 2025 highs; at peak euphoria with high burn, reducing emissions would not have been in anyone's top five concerns.
insights.speaker: Danny
insights.so_what: Signals these supply-side fixes are reactive to weak activity, a contrarian read that fundamentals discussion is itself a bearish-activity tell.
insights.quote: it's like cost-cutting for a business um during a downturn

insights.claim: The physical Pokemon/TCG card market is up nearly 2x in recent months, driven by Pokemon's 30th anniversary and serving as the current risk-on speculation vehicle, succeeding meme coins (fartcoin, SPX) as the way traders express risk appetite.
insights.speaker: Kunal
insights.so_what: Frames TCG/card exposure as a rotating beta of the broader risk-on cycle, useful as both a sentiment gauge and a momentum trade.
insights.quote: every market cycle people like to express their risk-on views in a in a certain way

insights.claim: Collector Crypt's on-chain gacha did ~$9M net revenue in May (record), with a ~10% net revenue margin, but unlike Hyperliquid it must spend most revenue acquiring ~$20M of physical card inventory, so little flows to the token even with buybacks.
insights.speaker: Kunal
insights.so_what: The inventory-cost structure and weak token value-accrual mean the ~4.5x multiple is not comparable to capital-light venues; discount the headline 'undervalued vs HYPE' narrative.
insights.quote: for every $10 people spend on spinning the machine, Collect-A-Crip takes home $1

insights.claim: About 66% of Collector Crypt users do not keep their cards and the average user is down ~3-5%, with most whales (one spent ~$22M) losing money, confirming the platform functions as a verifiable-odds casino with a small enduring house edge rather than a collector marketplace.
insights.speaker: Kunal
insights.so_what: Highlights the trade's reflexivity: whale-driven gambling volume will evaporate fast in a TCG drawdown, a key downside risk to the cards token and revenue.
insights.quote: almost 66% of people don't keep their cards

insights.claim: Collector Crypt built its own Oracle to price Pokemon cards, letting holders tokenize and sell illiquid cards back to the platform, sourcing inventory at a discount to fair value and overlapping with eBay-style trading.
insights.speaker: Kunal
insights.so_what: A genuine product moat (liquidity + discounted inventory acquisition) that could broaden the business beyond pure gacha if the card mania persists.

insights.claim: These momentum names (Pokemon cards, cards token) cannot be valued on fundamentals and resemble cyclical memory semiconductors trading at 5-6x PE despite triple-digit growth because the market prices the cyclicality, not the current run-rate.
insights.speaker: Danny
insights.so_what: Investors should treat cards exposure as a momentum/beta trade tied to broad risk-on, exiting when the TCG trend stalls rather than anchoring to a multiple.
insights.quote: you can't just say yeah let's just apply a 20 or 30 multiple to this
